ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chicken sausage food product class; no specific pan-fried Chinese chicken sausage entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers poultry meat sausages broadly; no specific pan-fried chicken sausage code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for pan-fried chicken sausage. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual prepared food products at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is one pan-fried chicken sausage link weighing approximately 80 g, as commonly served at Singapore kopitiam and bing sutt establishments.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken frankfurter / chicken sausage, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 07025 / FDC ID 172929) adapted with reference to USDA Foundation Foods chicken sausage entries
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No Singapore-specific HPB Foodo entry for pan-fried chicken sausage (生煎鸡肉肠) was found. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy chicken sausage / frankfurter (cooked) data, which represents a closely analogous processed chicken sausage product. Pan-frying adds a small additional fat contribution; values reflect a cooked, pan-fried state. Sodium is consistent with Chinese-style seasoned sausage products. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sausage and processed meat products — analogous estimate based on frankfurter/sausage GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for pan-fried chicken sausage (生煎鸡肉肠) was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 28 (low) based on analogous processed sausage and frankfurter products listed in the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently show low GI values (range 20–35) due to high protein and fat content with minimal starch. Glycemic load per 80 g serving calculated as (28 × 4.8 × 0.80) / 100 = 1.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~720 mg per 100 g; ~576 mg per 80 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of pan-fried chicken sausage due to its high sodium content, which can contribute significantly to daily sodium intake and elevate blood pressure; one serving provides approximately 25% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by health authorities.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index but high saturated fat and processed meat classification
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pan-fried chicken sausage has a low glycemic index (~28) and minimal carbohydrate content, so it does not cause rapid blood glucose spikes; however, its saturated fat content and processed meat classification warrant moderation for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, as regular processed meat consumption is associated with increased insulin resistance risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat, sodium, cholesterol, and processed meat classification
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pan-fried chicken sausage is a processed meat product with moderate saturated fat (~4.4 g/100 g), high sodium (~720 mg/100 g), and cholesterol (~75 mg/100 g); individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit intake, as regular processed meat consumption is independently associated with increased cardiovascular mortality risk per WHO and AHA guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pan-fried chicken sausage (生煎鸡肉肠, shēng jiān jī ròu cháng) is a Chinese-style processed poultry sausage made from seasoned ground chicken meat stuffed into a casing and cooked by pan-frying until the exterior is golden-brown and lightly crisp. It is commonly served at Singapore kopitiam (coffee shops) and bing sutt (ice rooms) establishments such as Keming Bing Sat, typically as a side dish or part of a set meal. The sausage is seasoned with soy sauce, garlic, ginger, white pepper, and salt, giving it a savoury, slightly smoky flavour with a juicy interior and a lightly caramelised crust from the pan-frying process. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy food (~218 kcal/100 g) with a meaningful protein contribution (~14.5 g/100 g) and moderate fat (~15.8 g/100 g), making it a filling protein source. Its sodium content is high (~720 mg/100 g), which is a primary clinical concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is low (~28), so it does not cause rapid postprandial glucose elevation, but its saturated fat and processed meat classification make it a food to moderate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular risk. It contains soy and gluten allergens from seasoning and casing ingredients. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal-certified products without verified halal sourcing.
common_user_queries: How many calories in pan-fried chicken sausage?
Is pan-fried chicken sausage high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry for pan-fried chicken sausage (生煎鸡肉肠) was found. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy chicken sausage data, which is a closely analogous processed chicken sausage product. GI is estimated from analogous sausage products in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to source adaptation rather than exact match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy chicken sausage source entry used for adaptation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. Vitamin C (vitamin_c_mg) is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — chicken sausage products contain no meaningful vitamin C. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.1 g/100 g as a trace amount consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in poultry products; confirmed trace per USDA data. Halal status is not confirmed; the input restaurant (Keming Bing Sat) is a Chinese kopitiam and halal certification is not verified. GI estimated at 28 (low) from analogous sausage products in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for this specific product was found. Nutrient values reflect a cooked, pan-fried state adapted from USDA SR Legacy data.
